profile.behavior: Often forages on the ground for ants and beetles; flies with deep flaps and glides, flashing the white rump and colored wing linings; excavates and uses tree cavities.
field_marks: Large, slim woodpecker with a rounded head, slightly downcurved pointed bill, and long tapered tail.
Brownish-gray upperparts finely barred black; pale underparts densely marked with round black spots.
Bold black crescent-shaped bib across the upper breast.
Bright white rump patch conspicuous in flight.
Underwings and underside of tail flash yellow or red, depending on form; males may show a black or red facial whisker.
profile.habitat: Open woodlands, forest edges, scattered-tree fields, burned or disturbed woodland, parks, and other open habitats near trees; also uses wet woodland edges.

sources.title: Northern Flicker Identification — Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: No material factual, species, or text errors found. The perched bird is a yellow-shafted adult male: black facial whisker, red nape crescent, barred brown-gray upperparts, black breast crescent, spotted pale underparts, and yellow underwing/tail coloration agree with the verified species accounts and the supplied references.

The plate depicts one complete bird with one head, one bill, two legs, one tail, and two wings (one largely occluded in the perched view); the isolated head and wing are clearly supplementary field-study details.
The white rump patch is described accurately but is not prominently exposed in the perched illustration; this is a minor presentation limitation, not a factual error.
All visible text claims match the verified identity, family, measurements, shape, plumage, behavior, and habitat. No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
